title: Two stacks in one array
description: Implement two stacks using a single array

examples.input: Initialize with n=6. Push 10 to Stack1, push 20 to Stack2, push 30 to Stack1, pop from Stack1, push 40 to Stack2, pop from Stack2
examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None, None, None, None, None], top1 = -1, top2 = 6, size = 6. Stack1 half: indices 0-2, Stack2 half: indices 3-5. Step 2: push1(10): Check top1 < 2? -1 < 2 yes. top1 becomes 0, arr[0] = 10. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, None], top1 = 0, top2 = 6. Step 3: push2(20): Check top2 > 3? 6 > 3 yes. top2 becomes 5, arr[5] = 20. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 5. Step 4: push1(30): Check top1 < 2? 0 < 2 yes. top1 becomes 1, arr[1] = 30. arr = [10, 30, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 1, top2 = 5. Step 5: pop1(): Check top1 >= 0? 1 >= 0 yes. x = arr[1] = 30, top1 becomes 0. Return 30. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 5. Step 6: push2(40): Check top2 > 3? 5 > 3 yes. top2 becomes 4, arr[4] = 40. arr = [10, None, None, None, 40, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 4. Step 7: pop2(): Check top2 < 6? 4 < 6 yes. x = arr[4] = 40, top2 becomes 5. Return 40. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 5.

examples.input: Initialize with n=4. Push 1 to Stack1, push 2 to Stack1, push 3 to Stack2, push 4 to Stack2, pop from Stack1, pop from Stack2
examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None, None, None], top1 = -1, top2 = 4, size = 4. Stack1 half: indices 0-1, Stack2 half: indices 2-3. Step 2: push1(1): Check top1 < 1? -1 < 1 yes. top1 becomes 0, arr[0] = 1. arr = [1, None, None, None], top1 = 0, top2 = 4. Step 3: push1(2): Check top1 < 1? 0 < 1 yes. top1 becomes 1, arr[1] = 2. arr = [1, 2, None, None], top1 = 1, top2 = 4. Step 4: push2(3): Check top2 > 2? 4 > 2 yes. top2 becomes 3, arr[3] = 3. arr = [1, 2, None, 3], top1 = 1, top2 = 3. Step 5: push2(4): Check top2 > 2? 3 > 2 yes. top2 becomes 2, arr[2] = 4. arr = [1, 2, 4, 3], top1 = 1, top2 = 2. Step 6: pop1(): Check top1 >= 0? 1 >= 0 yes. x = arr[1] = 2, top1 becomes 0. Return 2. arr = [1, None, 4, 3], top1 = 0, top2 = 2. Step 7: pop2(): Check top2 < 4? 2 < 4 yes. x = arr[2] = 4, top2 becomes 3. Return 4. arr = [1, None, None, 3], top1 = 0, top2 = 3.

approaches.name: Fixed Division Approach
approaches.summary: Divide the array into two equal halves, using the first half for stack1 and second half for stack2. Stack1 grows from left to right, stack2 grows from right to left within their respective halves.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Two Stacks Using Fixed Division Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of two stacks in one array by dividing it into two equal halves
ladders.explanation: The Fixed Division Approach solves the problem of implementing two stacks using a single array by dividing the array into two equal halves. Stack1 occupies the first half and grows from left to right, while Stack2 occupies the second half and grows from right to left within its half. This approach ensures O(1) time complexity for push and pop operations by maintaining separate top pointers for each stack. The intuition is that by fixing the boundaries and having stacks grow toward each other from opposite ends of their halves, we prevent overlap while maximizing space utilization within each half. The algorithm initializes an array of size n, sets top1 to -1 (indicating Stack1 is empty) and top2 to n (indicating Stack2 is empty, but note that Stack2's half starts at n/2, so top2 is initialized to n, not n/2, because it grows leftward). For push to Stack1, we check if top1 < (n/2)-1, then increment top1 and place the element. For push to Stack2, we check if top2 > n/2, then decrement top2 and place the element. For pop, we check if the respective stack is empty and return the element. Time complexity is O(1) for all operations as we only update pointers and access array indices. Space complexity is O(n) for the array. This feeds into the broader problem by providing a simple, efficient solution with fixed memory allocation. Common mistakes include incorrect boundary checks leading to stack overflow or underflow, and off-by-one errors in pointer initialization. For example, initializing top2 to n/2 instead of n would incorrectly limit Stack2's capacity. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: class TwoStacks: def __init__(self, n): self.arr = [None]*n; self.size = n; self.top1 = -1; self.top2 = n; def push1(self, x): if self.top1 < self.size//2 - 1: self.top1 += 1; self.arr[self.top1] = x; else: print('Stack1 Overflow'); def push2(self, x): if self.top2 > self.size//2: self.top2 -= 1; self.arr[self.top2] = x; else: print('Stack2 Overflow'); def pop1(self): if self.top1 >= 0: x = self.arr[self.top1]; self.top1 -= 1; return x; else: print('Stack1 Underflow'); return None; def pop2(self): if self.top2 < self.size: x = self.arr[self.top2]; self.top2 += 1; return x; else: print('Stack2 Underflow'); return None;

examples.input: Initialize with n=6. Push 10 to Stack1, push 20 to Stack2, push 30 to Stack1, pop from Stack1, push 40 to Stack2, pop from Stack2
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None, None, None, None, None], top1 = -1, top2 = 6, size = 6. Stack1 half: indices 0-2, Stack2 half: indices 3-5. Step 2: push1(10): Check top1 < 2? -1 < 2 yes. top1 becomes 0, arr[0] = 10. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, None], top1 = 0, top2 = 6. Step 3: push2(20): Check top2 > 3? 6 > 3 yes. top2 becomes 5, arr[5] = 20. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 5. Step 4: push1(30): Check top1 < 2? 0 < 2 yes. top1 becomes 1, arr[1] = 30. arr = [10, 30, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 1, top2 = 5. Step 5: pop1(): Check top1 >= 0? 1 >= 0 yes. x = arr[1] = 30, top1 becomes 0. Return 30. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 5. Step 6: push2(40): Check top2 > 3? 5 > 3 yes. top2 becomes 4, arr[4] = 40. arr = [10, None, None, None, 40, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 4. Step 7: pop2(): Check top2 < 6? 4 < 6 yes. x = arr[4] = 40, top2 becomes 5. Return 40. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, 20], top1 = 0, top2 = 5.

examples.input: Initialize with n=4. Push 1 to Stack1, push 2 to Stack1, push 3 to Stack2, push 4 to Stack2, pop from Stack1, pop from Stack2
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None, None, None], top1 = -1, top2 = 4, size = 4. Stack1 half: indices 0-1, Stack2 half: indices 2-3. Step 2: push1(1): Check top1 < 1? -1 < 1 yes. top1 becomes 0, arr[0] = 1. arr = [1, None, None, None], top1 = 0, top2 = 4. Step 3: push1(2): Check top1 < 1? 0 < 1 yes. top1 becomes 1, arr[1] = 2. arr = [1, 2, None, None], top1 = 1, top2 = 4. Return 2. arr = [1, None, 4, 3], top1 = 0, top2 = 2. Step 7: pop2(): Check top2 < 4? 2 < 4 yes. x = arr[2] = 4, top2 becomes 3. Return 4. arr = [1, None, None, 3], top1 = 0, top2 = 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Push and Pop Operations for a Single Stack in Fixed Half
ladders.desc: Implement push and pop for one stack within its fixed half of the array
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on implementing the push and pop operations for a single stack (e.g., Stack1) within its fixed half of the array. The intuition is that by allocating a specific contiguous block of the array to one stack, we can manage it using a top pointer that moves within that block. For Stack1, which grows left to right, the top pointer starts at -1 (indicating empty) and increments on push up to the end of its half. On pop, it decrements. The algorithm involves checking boundary conditions: for push, ensure top is less than the maximum index of the half to avoid overflow; for pop, ensure top is at least the starting index to avoid underflow. Time complexity is O(1) as we only update the pointer and access the array. Space complexity is O(1) extra space beyond the array. This feeds into L0 by being a core component of managing each stack independently. Common mistakes include incorrect boundary checks, such as using <= instead of < for overflow, or not updating the pointer correctly. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode for Stack1: class Stack1Half: def __init__(self, arr, start, end): self.arr = arr; self.start = start; self.end = end; self.top = start - 1; def push(self, x): if self.top < self.end: self.top += 1; self.arr[self.top] = x; else: print('Overflow'); def pop(self): if self.top >= self.start: x = self.arr[self.top]; self.top -= 1; return x; else: print('Underflow'); return None;

examples.input: Initialize array of size 6, Stack1 half indices 0-2. Push 10, push 20, pop, push 30
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None, None, None, None, None], start=0, end=2, top = -1. Step 2: push(10): Check top < 2? -1 < 2 yes. top becomes 0, arr[0] = 10. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, None], top=0. Step 3: push(20): Check top < 2? 0 < 2 yes. top becomes 1, arr[1] = 20. arr = [10, 20, None, None, None, None], top=1. Step 4: pop(): Check top >= 0? 1 >= 0 yes. x = arr[1] = 20, top becomes 0. Return 20. arr = [10, None, None, None, None, None], top=0. Step 5: push(30): Check top < 2? 0 < 2 yes. top becomes 1, arr[1] = 30. arr = [10, 30, None, None, None, None], top=1.

examples.input: Initialize array of size 4, Stack1 half indices 0-1. Push 5, push 6, push 7 [should overflow]
examples.output: After operations: arr[0]=5, arr[1]=6, overflow on push 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None, None, None], start=0, end=1, top = -1. Step 2: push(5): Check top < 1? -1 < 1 yes. top becomes 0, arr[0] = 5. arr = [5, None, None, None], top=0. Step 3: push(6): Check top < 1? 0 < 1 yes. top becomes 1, arr[1] = 6. arr = [5, 6, None, None], top=1. Step 4: push(7): Check top < 1? 1 < 1 no. Print 'Overflow'. arr remains [5, 6, None, None], top=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Managing Two Top Pointers in Opposite Directions
ladders.desc: Coordinate two top pointers moving in opposite directions within fixed halves
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves managing two top pointers that move in opposite directions within their respective fixed halves of the array. For Stack1, the top pointer (top1) starts at -1 and increments on push, moving rightward. For Stack2, the top pointer (top2) starts at n (where n is array size) and decrements on push, moving leftward within its half. The intuition is that by having pointers move toward each other from the ends of their halves, we maximize space usage without overlap. The algorithm requires initializing top1 and top2 correctly and updating them independently during push and pop operations. Boundary checks are crucial: for Stack1, push is allowed if top1 < (n/2)-1; for Stack2, push is allowed if top2 > n/2. This ensures pointers stay within their halves. Time complexity is O(1) for pointer updates. Space complexity is O(1) extra. This feeds into L1 by combining two instances of single-stack management. Common mistakes include initializing top2 incorrectly (e.g., to n/2) or mixing up inequality signs in checks. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: top1 = -1; top2 = n; def push1(x): if top1 < n//2 - 1: top1 += 1; arr[top1] = x; else: overflow; def push2(x): if top2 > n//2: top2 -= 1; arr[top2] = x; else: overflow;

examples.input: Array size n=6. Initialize pointers. Push 10 to Stack1, push 20 to Stack2
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize n=6, arr = [None, None, None, None, None, None], top1 = -1, top2 = 6. Stack1 half: indices 0-2, Stack2 half: indices 3-5. Step 2: push1(10): Check top1 < 2? -1 < 2 yes. top1 becomes 0, arr[0] = 10. top1=0, top2=6. Step 3: push2(20): Check top2 > 3? 6 > 3 yes. top2 becomes 5, arr[5] = 20. top1=0, top2=5.

examples.input: Array size n=4. Initialize pointers. Push 1 to Stack1, push 2 to Stack2, push 3 to Stack1
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize n=4, arr = [None, None, None, None], top1 = -1, top2 = 4. Stack1 half: indices 0-1, Stack2 half: indices 2-3. Step 2: push1(1): Check top1 < 1? -1 < 1 yes. top1 becomes 0, arr[0] = 1. top1=0, top2=4. Step 3: push2(2): Check top2 > 2? 4 > 2 yes. top2 becomes 3, arr[3] = 2. top1=0, top2=3. Step 4: push1(3): Check top1 < 1? 0 < 1 yes. top1 becomes 1, arr[1] = 3. top1=1, top2=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing with Pointer Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Use pointer arithmetic to access and update array elements for stack operations
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using pointer arithmetic to access and update array elements based on a top pointer. The intuition is that a stack can be implemented in an array by maintaining an index (pointer) that points to the top element. When pushing, we increment the pointer and store the element at that index; when popping, we retrieve the element and decrement the pointer. The algorithm requires understanding how array indices work: they start at 0, and we can move the pointer within a fixed range. For example, if the stack occupies indices from start to end, the pointer starts at start-1 (empty) and moves to end at full. Time complexity is O(1) for accessing array via index. Space complexity is O(1) extra. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to manipulate the array based on pointer positions. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices or not updating the pointer before/after accessing the array. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: pointer = start - 1; def push(x): if pointer < end: pointer += 1; arr[pointer] = x; def pop(): if pointer >= start: x = arr[pointer]; pointer -= 1; return x;

examples.input: Array arr = [None, None, None], start=0, end=2. Push 10, push 20, pop
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None, None], start=0, end=2, pointer = -1. Step 2: push(10): Check pointer < 2? -1 < 2 yes. pointer becomes 0, arr[0] = 10. arr = [10, None, None], pointer=0. Step 3: push(20): Check pointer < 2? 0 < 2 yes. pointer becomes 1, arr[1] = 20. arr = [10, 20, None], pointer=1. Step 4: pop(): Check pointer >= 0? 1 >= 0 yes. x = arr[1] = 20, pointer becomes 0. Return 20. arr = [10, None, None], pointer=0.

examples.input: Array arr = [None, None], start=0, end=1. Push 5, pop, push 6
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr = [None, None], start=0, end=1, pointer = -1. Step 2: push(5): Check pointer < 1? -1 < 1 yes. pointer becomes 0, arr[0] = 5. arr = [5, None], pointer=0. Step 3: pop(): Check pointer >= 0? 0 >= 0 yes. x = arr[0] = 5, pointer becomes -1. Return 5. arr = [None, None], pointer=-1. Step 4: push(6): Check pointer < 1? -1 < 1 yes. pointer becomes 0, arr[0] = 6. arr = [6, None], pointer=0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Stack Empty/Full Conditions
ladders.desc: Determine if a stack is empty or full based on pointer position
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves checking whether a stack is empty or full by examining the position of its top pointer relative to fixed boundaries. For a stack implemented in an array with indices from start to end, the stack is empty if the pointer is less than start (typically pointer = start - 1). It is full if the pointer equals end. The intuition is that the pointer tracks the current top element; if it hasn't reached the start index, no elements are present; if it has reached the end index, no more space is available. The algorithm requires comparing the pointer with start and end values. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the condition checks needed before push or pop operations. Common mistakes include using incorrect comparison operators (e.g., <= instead of == for full) or not accounting for initial empty state. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def isEmpty(pointer, start): return pointer < start; def isFull(pointer, end): return pointer == end;

examples.input: Pointer = -1, start=0, end=2. Check empty and full.
examples.trace: Step 1: Given pointer = -1, start=0, end=2. Step 2: Check empty: pointer < start? -1 < 0 yes, so empty is True. Step 3: Check full: pointer == end? -1 == 2 no, so full is False.

examples.input: After pushing two items, pointer=1, start=0, end=2. Check empty and full.
examples.trace: Step 1: Given pointer = 1, start=0, end=2. Step 2: Check empty: pointer < start? 1 < 0 no, so empty is False. Step 3: Check full: pointer == end? 1 == 2 no, so full is False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array as Contiguous Memory Block
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as fixed-size contiguous blocks of memory with index-based access
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays as fixed-size contiguous blocks of memory where each element can be accessed using an index. The intuition is that an array allocates a sequence of memory locations, each holding a value, and we can refer to them by positions starting from 0. For example, an array of size n has indices 0 to n-1. This is crucial for implementing stacks because we need a structured way to store multiple elements and quickly access the top. The algorithm involves declaring an array, initializing it (e.g., with None or zeros), and using indices to read or write values. Time complexity for access is O(1) since indexing is direct. Space complexity is O(n) for the array itself. This feeds into L4 by providing the basic data structure on which stack operations are built. Common mistakes include accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., index n in an array of size n) or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: arr = [None] * n;  # Create array of size n with None values. Access element at index i: arr[i]. Update element: arr[i] = value.

examples.input: Create array of size 3. Set arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, access arr[0] and arr[2].
examples.trace: Step 1: Create array: arr = [None, None, None]. Step 2: Set arr[0] = 10: arr becomes [10, None, None]. Step 3: Set arr[1] = 20: arr becomes [10, 20, None]. Step 4: Access arr[0]: returns 10. Step 5: Access arr[2]: returns None.

examples.input: Array of size 2. Initialize all to 0. Update arr[1] to 5.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create array: arr = [0, 0]. Step 2: Update arr[1] = 5: arr becomes [0, 5].

testCases.input: Array size 4, set arr[0]=1, arr[2]=3, access arr[1]
testCases.expected: arr[1] is None or 0 if initialized

approaches.name: Space Efficient Approach
approaches.summary: Both stacks grow towards each other from opposite ends of the array. Stack1 starts from index 0 and grows rightwards, stack2 starts from last index and grows leftwards.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Two Stacks in One Array Using Space-Efficient Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of two stacks sharing a single array where stacks grow towards each other from opposite ends.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires implementing two independent stack data structures using a single fixed-size array. The space-efficient approach positions stack1 at the beginning of the array (index 0) growing rightwards, and stack2 at the end of the array (last index) growing leftwards. This allows both stacks to utilize available space dynamically without pre-allocating fixed halves. The intuition is that by having the stacks grow towards each other, we maximize array utilization—when one stack is small, the other can use more space. We maintain two pointers: top1 for stack1 (initially -1, increments on push) and top2 for stack2 (initially array size, decrements on push). Push operations check for overflow (when top1 + 1 == top2), and pop operations check for underflow (top1 == -1 for stack1 or top2 == array size for stack2). Time complexity is O(1) for all operations as we're just updating pointers and accessing array indices. Space complexity is O(n) for the array storage. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) by combining the push/pop logic for both stacks with overflow/underflow handling. Common mistakes include not checking overflow/underflow conditions properly, mixing up pointer directions, or not updating pointers correctly after operations. Real code pattern: class TwoStacks: def __init__(self, n): self.arr = [None]*n; self.top1 = -1; self.top2 = n; def push1(x): if self.top1 + 1 < self.top2: self.top1 += 1; self.arr[self.top1] = x; else: raise Exception('Stack Overflow'); similarly for others.

examples.input: Initialize array size 6, operations: push1(10), push2(20), push1(30), push2(40), pop1(), pop2()
examples.output: pop1 returns 30, pop2 returns 40, final array state: [10, None, None, None, 20, None] with top1=0, top2=4
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr=[None,None,None,None,None,None], top1=-1, top2=6. Step 2: push1(10): check top1+1=0 < top2=6 → top1=0, arr[0]=10. Step 3: push2(20): check top2-1=5 > top1=0 → top2=5, arr[5]=20. Step 4: push1(30): check top1+1=1 < top2=5 → top1=1, arr[1]=30. Step 5: push2(40): check top2-1=4 > top1=1 → top2=4, arr[4]=40. Step 6: pop1(): check top1>=0 → return arr[1]=30, top1=0. Step 7: pop2(): check top2<6 → return arr[4]=40, top2=5.

examples.input: Initialize array size 4, operations: push1(5), push2(15), push1(25), push2(35), push1(45)
examples.output: Last push1(45) causes Stack Overflow, final array state: [5,25,15,35] with top1=1, top2=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr=[None,None,None,None], top1=-1, top2=4. Step 2: push1(5): top1+1=0 < top2=4 → top1=0, arr[0]=5. Step 3: push2(15): top2-1=3 > top1=0 → top2=3, arr[3]=15. Step 4: push1(25): top1+1=1 < top2=3 → top1=1, arr[1]=25. Step 5: push2(35): top2-1=2 > top1=1 → top2=2, arr[2]=35. Step 6: push1(45): top1+1=2 is NOT < top2=2 → Stack Overflow, operation fails.

testCases.input: size=5, ops: push1(1), push2(2), pop1(), pop2()
size=4, ops: push2(100), push2(200), push1(50), pop2()
size=2, ops: push1(5), push2(10), pop1(), push2(15)
size=6, ops: push1(1), push2(6), push1(2), push2(5), push1(3), push2(4)
size=4, ops: push1(10), push2(20), push1(30), push2(40), pop1(), pop1(), pop2()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Push Operation for Both Stacks with Overflow Detection
ladders.desc: Implement the push operation for both stacks that checks for overflow when stacks meet in the middle.
ladders.explanation: The push operation is the most critical subroutine because it manages how elements are added to either stack while preventing overflow. For stack1 (growing rightwards from index 0), we increment top1 and store the value at arr[top1]. For stack2 (growing leftwards from the last index), we decrement top2 and store at arr[top2]. The key insight is that overflow occurs when the next available position for stack1 (top1+1) equals the next available position for stack2 (top2), meaning the stacks have met and no space remains. This is more efficient than pre-splitting the array because it dynamically allocates space. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves a comparison and array assignment. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This feeds into L0 by being the core operation that, combined with pop, forms the complete solution. Common mistakes include not checking overflow before updating pointers, updating pointers in the wrong direction, or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: def push1(x): if self.top1 + 1 < self.top2: self.top1 += 1; self.arr[self.top1] = x; else: raise Exception('Stack Overflow'); def push2(x): if self.top2 - 1 > self.top1: self.top2 -= 1; self.arr[self.top2] = x; else: raise Exception('Stack Overflow').

examples.input: Array size 4, current state: top1=0, top2=3, arr=[5,None,None,10], push1(15)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check overflow: top1+1=1 < top2=3 → condition true. Step 2: Increment top1: top1=1. Step 3: Assign arr[1]=15. Step 4: New state: arr=[5,15,None,10].

examples.input: Array size 5, current state: top1=1, top2=4, arr=[1,2,None,5,None], push2(4)
examples.output: Stack Overflow when stacks approach each other
examples.trace: Step 1: Check overflow for push2: top2-1=3 > top1=1? Yes, 3 > 1 is true. Step 2: Decrement top2: top2=3, arr[3]=4 assigned. Step 3: Continue with next push. To show overflow: try push2(5) next: top2-1=2 > top1=1? Yes, true. top2=2, arr[2]=5. Step 4: Now try push2(6): top2-1=1 > top1=1? No, 1 > 1 is false. Overflow occurs. This demonstrates how stacks meet at the boundary.

testCases.input: size=2, state: top1=-1, top2=2, push1(90), push2(100)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointer Management for Stacks Growing Towards Each Other
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the pointer movement logic that allows two stacks to share array space efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The core logic revolves around managing two pointers, top1 and top2, that represent the top elements of stack1 and stack2 respectively. Stack1 starts at index 0 and grows rightwards: top1 initializes to -1 (indicating empty), and on push, it increments to 0,1,2,...; on pop, it decrements. Stack2 starts at the last index (size-1) and grows leftwards: top2 initializes to array size (one past the end for empty), and on push, it decrements to size-1, size-2,...; on pop, it increments. The intuition is that by moving towards each other, the pointers naturally partition the array: all indices <= top1 belong to stack1, all indices >= top2 belong to stack2, and the middle region is free space. This dynamic partitioning maximizes space usage compared to a fixed split. Time and space analysis: pointer updates are O(1) time, O(1) extra space. This connects to L1 as the foundation for push/pop operations. Common mistakes: initializing top2 incorrectly (should be array size, not size-1), confusing increment/decrement directions, or not updating pointers after pop. Real code pattern: Initialize: top1 = -1; top2 = n; Push1: top1 += 1; arr[top1] = x; Push2: top2 -= 1; arr[top2] = x; Pop1: val = arr[top1]; top1 -= 1; return val; Pop2: val = arr[top2]; top2 += 1; return val.

examples.input: Array size 5, initial state: top1=-1, top2=5. Perform: push1(10), push2(20), push1(30)
examples.trace: Step 1: push1(10): top1 increments -1→0, arr[0]=10. Step 2: push2(20): top2 decrements 5→4, arr[4]=20. Step 3: push1(30): top1 increments 0→1, arr[1]=30. Final: top1=1, top2=4.

examples.input: Array size 3, state: top1=0, top2=3, arr=[5,None,None]. Perform: pop1(), push2(15)
examples.trace: Step 1: pop1(): return arr[0]=5, top1 decrements 0→-1. Step 2: push2(15): top2 decrements 3→2, arr[2]=15. Final: top1=-1, top2=2.

testCases.input: size=4, init: top1=-1, top2=4, push1(1), push2(4)
size=5, init: top1=-1, top2=5, push1(10), push1(20), push2(30)
size=5, state: top1=2, top2=3, pop1(), pop2()
size=3, state: top1=0, top2=3, push2(40), pop1()

testCases.expected: top1=2 (overflow if checked, but pointer logic would update to 2)

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Master accessing array elements with indices and ensuring operations stay within valid bounds.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on fundamental array operations: reading from and writing to specific indices, and checking that indices are within the array bounds [0, n-1] where n is the array size. In the two-stacks problem, we use indices like top1 and top2 to access elements, so understanding zero-based indexing is crucial. For stack1, valid indices range from 0 to top1 (when not empty); for stack2, from top2 to n-1. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access time, but going outside bounds causes errors. We must check conditions: before pushing, ensure the new index is within bounds and doesn't conflict with the other stack; before popping, ensure the stack is not empty (index is valid). Time complexity for indexing is O(1), space is O(1). This connects to L2 as it enables pointer-based access. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using index n instead of n-1), not handling empty stacks (e.g., top1=-1), or confusing array length with last index. Real code pattern: To access arr[i], ensure 0 <= i < len(arr). For stack1 empty: top1 == -1; for stack2 empty: top2 == len(arr). For push1: new index = top1 + 1 must be < len(arr) and < top2.

examples.input: Array arr = [10,20,30,40] of size 4. Access arr[2], then try arr[4].
examples.trace: Step 1: Check bounds for index 2: 0 <= 2 < 4 → true, so access arr[2]=30. Step 2: Check bounds for index 4: 0 <= 4 < 4 → false, so invalid, typical error.

examples.input: Array size 5, top1=3, top2=5. Check if push1 to index 4 is valid.
examples.output: Valid if 4 < top2 (5) and 4 < array size (5), so yes.
examples.trace: Step 1: New index for push1 = top1+1 = 4. Step 2: Check 4 < array size 5 → true. Step 3: Also check 4 < top2=5 → true. So valid.

testCases.input: size=6, top1=2, check if index 3 is within bounds
size=6, top2=3, check if index 2 is within bounds for stack2
size=4, top1=0, top2=4, check if index 1 is valid for push1
size=4, top1=2, top2=3, check if index 3 is valid for push1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Push and Pop on a Single Stack
ladders.desc: Implement push (add to top) and pop (remove from top) operations for a standard stack using an array.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental stack operations using an array as the underlying storage. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure. We maintain a pointer 'top' that tracks the index of the top element. Initially, top = -1 indicating an empty stack. Push operation: increment top, then store the value at arr[top]. Pop operation: retrieve arr[top], then decrement top. The intuition is that we're always adding and removing from the same end (the top), which makes these operations O(1). Time complexity: O(1) for both push and pop. Space complexity: O(n) for the array. This connects to L3 as it extends single-stack operations to two stacks by managing multiple pointers. Common mistakes: not checking for stack overflow (when top == array size -1) on push, or stack underflow (when top == -1) on pop; also, forgetting to update the pointer after operations. Real code pattern: class Stack: def __init__(self, n): self.arr = [None]*n; self.top = -1; def push(x): if self.top < len(self.arr)-1: self.top += 1; self.arr[self.top] = x; else: raise Exception('Overflow'); def pop(): if self.top >= 0: val = self.arr[self.top]; self.top -= 1; return val; else: raise Exception('Underflow').

examples.input: Stack size 3, operations: push(10), push(20), pop(), push(30)
examples.output: After ops: arr=[10,30,None], top=1, pop returned 20
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr=[None,None,None], top=-1. Step 2: push(10): top=-1→0, arr[0]=10. Step 3: push(20): top=0→1, arr[1]=20. Step 4: pop(): return arr[1]=20, top=1→0. Step 5: push(30): top=0→1, arr[1]=30.

examples.input: Stack size 2, operations: push(5), push(10), push(15)
examples.output: Third push causes Overflow, final arr=[5,10], top=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr=[None,None], top=-1. Step 2: push(5): top=-1→0, arr[0]=5. Step 3: push(10): top=0→1, arr[1]=10. Step 4: push(15): check top=1 < size-1=1? No → Overflow.

testCases.input: size=3, ops: push(50), push(60), push(70), push(80)
size=6, ops: push(3), push(4), pop(), push(5), pop()

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Index-Based Access
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as contiguous memory blocks where elements are accessed using indices.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element is accessed by an index, typically starting from 0. For example, in an array of size n, valid indices are 0, 1, 2, ..., n-1. The key intuition is that arrays provide O(1) random access time because the memory address of any element can be calculated directly from the base address and index. This efficiency makes arrays ideal for implementing stacks where we need fast push/pop operations. In programming, we declare arrays with a size, and we can read or write values at specific indices. Time complexity for access is O(1), space is O(n) for storing n elements. This connects to L4 as it provides the storage mechanism for stack operations. Common mistakes: using indices outside the valid range (causing errors), confusing array size with last index, or not initializing array elements. Real code pattern: In Python-like pseudocode, arr = [None]*5 creates an array of size 5 with all None; arr[0] = 10 assigns value 10 to first element; x = arr[2] reads third element. Always check 0 <= index < len(arr) before access.

examples.input: Create array of size 3 with values [5, 10, 15]. Access index 1, then set index 2 to 20.
examples.output: Access index 1 gives 10; after set, array becomes [5,10,20]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize arr = [5,10,15]. Step 2: Access arr[1]: check index 1 is between 0 and 2 → true, so value is 10. Step 3: Set arr[2] = 20: check index 2 is valid → true, so arr becomes [5,10,20].

examples.input: Array size 4, all None. Try to access index 4.
examples.trace: Step 1: arr = [None,None,None,None]. Step 2: Attempt to access arr[4]: check 0 <= 4 < 4? 4 < 4 is false, so invalid index.

testCases.input: size=5, create array of None, set index 2 to 99
arr=[7,8,9], check if index -1 is valid

approaches.name: Dynamic Resizing Approach
approaches.summary: Implement the space efficient approach but with dynamic array resizing when either stack reaches capacity. When resizing occurs, redistribute elements to maintain the two-stack structure.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized for push/pop, O(n) worst-case for resize

ladders.title: Full Problem — Two Stacks with Dynamic Resizing
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of two stacks in one array with dynamic resizing when capacity is reached.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements two stacks (Stack1 from left, Stack2 from right) in a single array with dynamic resizing. The intuition is to maximize space efficiency while maintaining O(1) amortized time for push/pop operations. When either stack reaches capacity, we resize the array (typically doubling it) and redistribute elements to maintain the two-stack structure with equal spacing. The algorithm initializes an array with initial capacity, tracks top indices for both stacks, and implements push/pop operations with boundary checks. When resizing is needed, we create a new larger array, copy Stack1 elements starting from index 0, copy Stack2 elements starting from the new array's end, and update the array reference and top indices. Time complexity is O(1) amortized for push/pop (due to occasional O(n) resizing), and O(n) worst-case for resize. Space complexity is O(n) for the array. This connects to L1 by using the resize function when capacity is exhausted. Common mistakes include not updating both top indices after resize, incorrect boundary checks leading to stack overflow/underflow, and inefficient resizing strategies. Real code pattern: Initialize array, track top1 and top2, implement push/pop with checks, call resize when needed.

examples.input: Initialize with capacity 4. Push to Stack1: 1, 2. Push to Stack2: 3, 4. Push to Stack1: 5.
examples.output: Array after operations: [1, 2, 5, None, None, None, 4, 3] (after single resize to capacity 8)
examples.trace: 1. Initialize array = [None]*4, top1 = -1, top2 = 4. 2. Push 1 to Stack1: top1=0, array[0]=1. 3. Push 2 to Stack1: top1=1, array[1]=2. 4. Push 3 to Stack2: top2=3, array[3]=3. 5. Push 4 to Stack2: top2=2, array[2]=4. 6. Check push 5 to Stack1: top1+1 == top2? 2 == 2 → overflow detected. Resize to capacity 8: new_array = [None]*8. Copy Stack1 [0:2]: new_array[0]=1, new_array[1]=2. Copy Stack2 (2 elements from old indices 2-3): new_array[6]=4, new_array[7]=3. Update: top1=1, top2=6. 7. Push 5 to Stack1: top1=2, array[2]=5. Final: [1,2,5,None,None,None,4,3], top1=2, top2=6.

examples.input: Initialize with capacity 2. Push to Stack1: 10. Push to Stack2: 20. Pop from Stack1. Push to Stack2: 30.
examples.output: Array after operations: [30, 20] (no resize needed, array has sufficient space)
examples.trace: 1. Initialize array = [None]*2, top1 = -1, top2 = 2. 2. Push 10 to Stack1: check -1+1 < 2 → 0 < 2 yes, top1=0, array[0]=10. 3. Push 20 to Stack2: check 2-1 > 0 → 1 > 0 yes, top2=1, array[1]=20. Current: [10,20], top1=0, top2=1 (full). 4. Pop from Stack1: array[0]=10 retrieved, top1=-1. Current: [?,20], top1=-1, top2=1. 5. Push 30 to Stack2: check 1-1 > -1 → 0 > -1 yes, top2=0, array[0]=30. Final: [30,20], top1=-1, top2=0. No resize occurred.

testCases.input: Initialize cap=2. Push Stack1: 1. Push Stack2: 2. Pop Stack1. Pop Stack2.
Initialize cap=3. Push Stack1: 1,2. Push Stack2: 3. Push Stack1: 4.
Initialize cap=4. Push Stack1: 1,2. Push Stack2: 3,4. Pop Stack1. Pop Stack2.
Initialize cap=1. Push Stack1: 10. Push Stack2: 20.
Initialize cap=5. Push Stack1: 1,2,3. Push Stack2: 4,5. Pop Stack1 twice. Push Stack2: 6.
Initialize cap=2. Push Stack1: 1. Push Stack2: 2. Push Stack1: 3. Push Stack2: 4.
Initialize cap=3. Push Stack1: 1. Pop Stack1. Push Stack2: 2. Pop Stack2.
Initialize cap=4. Push Stack1: 1,2,3. Push Stack2: 4. Pop Stack2. Push Stack1: 5.
Initialize cap=2. Push Stack1: 1. Push Stack1: 2. Push Stack2: 3.
Initialize cap=3. Push Stack2: 1,2. Push Stack1: 3. Pop Stack2. Push Stack1: 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Dynamic Array Resizing Function
ladders.desc: Implement the resize function that doubles array capacity and redistributes stack elements.
ladders.explanation: The resize function is called when either stack reaches capacity (i.e., top1 + 1 == top2). The intuition is to allocate a larger array (typically double the current capacity) and redistribute elements to maintain the two-stack structure with balanced spacing. This ensures amortized O(1) time for push operations despite occasional O(n) resizing. The algorithm: 1. Create a new array with double capacity. 2. Copy elements from Stack1 (indices 0 to top1) to the start of the new array. 3. Copy elements from Stack2 (indices top2 to old capacity-1) to the end of the new array, maintaining their order. 4. Update the array reference and top indices (top1 remains same, top2 = new capacity - (old capacity - old top2)). Time complexity is O(n) for copying elements. Space complexity is O(n) for the new array. This feeds into L0 by being called during push operations when capacity is full. Common mistakes include incorrect calculation of new top2, not preserving element order during copy, and inefficient resizing factor. Real code pattern: def resize(): new_cap = 2*old_cap; new_arr = [None]*new_cap; copy Stack1 to new_arr[0:top1+1]; copy Stack2 to new_arr[new_cap-(old_cap-top2):new_cap]; update array and top2.

examples.input: Array = [1,2,None,3,4], top1=1, top2=3, capacity=5. Resize triggered.
examples.trace: 1. Old capacity=5, new capacity=10. 2. New array = [None]*10. 3. Copy Stack1: indices 0-1 have [1,2] to new array indices 0-1. 4. Copy Stack2: old indices 3-4 have [3,4] to new array indices 9-8 (to maintain order: new index = new_capacity - (old_capacity - old_index)). Calculate: for old index 3: new index = 10 - (5-3) = 8; for old index 4: new index = 10 - (5-4) = 9. So new array[8]=3, new_array[9]=4. 5. Update top2 = new_capacity - (old_capacity - old_top2) = 10 - (5-3) = 8. But since Stack2 elements are at indices 8 and 9, top2 should point to first element (index 8). Wait, correction: top2 should be 8 (pointing to index 8 where 3 is). Actually, in implementation, top2 typically points to the next available spot or the top element; here assuming it points to the top element (index 4 originally), so after copy, top2 = 9 (pointing to 4). Let's clarify: If top2 points to the top element (4 at index 4), then after resize, top2 = 9. If it points to next available spot (index 3 originally), then top2 = 8. For consistency, we'll use top2 as next available spot from right: originally top2=3, after resize top2=8.

examples.input: Array = [10,None,20], top1=0, top2=2, capacity=3. Resize triggered.
examples.trace: 1. Old capacity=3, new capacity=6. 2. New array = [None]*6. 3. Copy Stack1: index 0 has 10 to new array index 0. 4. Copy Stack2: old index 2 has 20 to new array index 5 (new index = 6 - (3-2) = 5). 5. Update top2 = new_capacity - (old_capacity - old_top2) = 6 - (3-2) = 5. Final array: indices 0:10, 5:20, others None.

testCases.expected: New array length 12, elements at indices 0-2:1,2,3 and indices 10-11:5,4
New array length 8, elements at indices 6-7:2,1
New array length 10, elements at indices 0-1:5,6 and indices 8-9:8,7
New array length 4, element at index 0:1
New array length 6, elements at indices 5-4:3,2
New array length 14, elements at indices 0-3:1,2,3,4 and indices 12-13:6,5
New array length 0 (edge case, but typically initialize with min capacity)
New array length 10, elements at indices 0-2:10,20,30 and indices 9:40
New array length 8, elements at indices 0-1:1,2 and indices 7:3
New array length 8, elements at index 0:5 and index 7:6

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two Stacks in One Array with Boundary Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement two stacks in a fixed-size array by growing from opposite ends and tracking top indices.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to use a single array to store two stacks by having one stack grow from the left (index 0) and the other from the right (end of array). This maximizes space utilization until the array is full. The intuition is that by separating the growth directions, we can efficiently use the array without shifting elements. The algorithm: Initialize an array of fixed capacity, set top1 = -1 (for Stack1 growing left to right), and top2 = capacity (for Stack2 growing right to left). For push to Stack1: increment top1, store element at array[top1]. For push to Stack2: decrement top2, store element at array[top2]. Check for overflow when top1 + 1 == top2. For pop: check underflow (top1 == -1 for Stack1, top2 == capacity for Stack2), retrieve element, and update top index. Time complexity is O(1) for all operations. Space complexity is O(n). This feeds into L1 by providing the base structure before adding dynamic resizing. Common mistakes include incorrect initial values for top indices, off-by-one errors in boundary checks, and not handling empty stacks properly. Real code pattern: class TwoStacks: def __init__(cap): self.arr = [None]*cap; self.top1 = -1; self.top2 = cap; def push1(val): if self.top1 + 1 == self.top2: raise error; self.top1 += 1; self.arr[self.top1] = val.

examples.input: Capacity=5. Push Stack1: 1,2. Push Stack2: 3,4. Pop Stack1. Push Stack2: 5.
examples.trace: 1. Initialize arr=[None]*5, top1=-1, top2=5. 2. Push 1 to Stack1: top1=0, arr[0]=1. 3. Push 2 to Stack1: top1=1, arr[1]=2. 4. Push 3 to Stack2: top2=4, arr[4]=3. 5. Push 4 to Stack2: top2=3, arr[3]=4. 6. Pop Stack1: value=2, top1=0. 7. Push 5 to Stack2: top2=2, arr[2]=5. Final arr: indices 0:1, 1:None (from pop), 2:5, 3:4, 4:3.

examples.input: Capacity=3. Push Stack1: 10. Push Stack2: 20. Push Stack1: 30.
examples.trace: 1. Initialize arr=[None]*3, top1=-1, top2=3. 2. Push 10 to Stack1: top1=0, arr[0]=10. 3. Push 20 to Stack2: top2=2, arr[2]=20. 4. Push 30 to Stack1: top1=1, but check: top1+1=2, top2=2, so overflow. Cannot push.

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Implementation with Array
ladders.desc: Implement a single stack using an array with push and pop operations.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves implementing a stack data structure using an array as the underlying storage. The intuition is that a stack follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) order, and an array provides contiguous memory for efficient access. The algorithm: Initialize an array with some capacity and a top index (typically -1 for empty stack). For push: check if stack is full (top == capacity-1), if not, increment top and store element at array[top]. For pop: check if stack is empty (top == -1), if not, retrieve element at array[top], decrement top, and return element. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space complexity is O(n). This feeds into L2 by being the fundamental unit for each of the two stacks. Common mistakes include not checking for stack full/empty conditions, incorrect top initialization, and off-by-one errors in capacity checks. Real code pattern: class Stack: def __init__(cap): self.arr = [None]*cap; self.top = -1; self.cap = cap; def push(val): if self.top == self.cap-1: raise error; self.top += 1; self.arr[self.top] = val; def pop(): if self.top == -1: raise error; val = self.arr[self.top]; self.top -= 1; return val.

examples.input: Capacity=3. Push: 10, 20, 30. Pop. Push: 40.
examples.trace: 1. Initialize arr=[None]*3, top=-1. 2. Push 10: top=0, arr[0]=10. 3. Push 20: top=1, arr[1]=20. 4. Push 30: top=2, arr[2]=30. 5. Pop: value=30, top=1. 6. Push 40: top=2, arr[2]=40. Final arr: indices 0:10, 1:20, 2:40.

examples.input: Capacity=2. Push: 1. Pop. Pop.
examples.trace: 1. Initialize arr=[None]*2, top=-1. 2. Push 1: top=0, arr[0]=1. 3. Pop: value=1, top=-1. 4. Pop: top==-1, so underflow error.

testCases.input: Cap=3. Push: 1. Pop. Push: 2. Pop. Push: 3.
Cap=2. Push: 1. Push: 2. Pop. Pop. Push: 3.
Cap=3. Push: 1. Push: 2. Pop. Push: 3. Pop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Perform operations on arrays with proper index management and boundary validation.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing and modifying array elements using indices, while ensuring indices are within valid bounds (0 to length-1). The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access time via indices, but incorrect indices can lead to errors or undefined behavior. The algorithm: For an array of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. To access an element: check if index >= 0 and index < n, then use array[index]. To modify: similarly check bounds, then assign array[index] = value. Time complexity is O(1) for access/modify. Space complexity is O(1) additional. This feeds into L3 as arrays are used to implement stacks. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using index n for length n array), negative indices without proper checks, and not validating input indices. Real code pattern: def access(arr, idx): if idx < 0 or idx >= len(arr): raise IndexError; return arr[idx]; def modify(arr, idx, val): if idx < 0 or idx >= len(arr): raise IndexError; arr[idx] = val.

examples.input: Array = [10,20,30,40]. Access index 2. Modify index 1 to 25.
examples.trace: 1. Array length=4, valid indices 0-3. 2. Access index 2: check 2>=0 and 2<4, true, so return arr[2]=30. 3. Modify index 1 to 25: check 1>=0 and 1<4, true, so arr[1]=25. Final array: [10,25,30,40].

examples.input: Array = [5,6,7]. Access index 3.
examples.trace: 1. Array length=3, valid indices 0-2. 2. Access index 3: check 3>=0 true, but 3<3 false, so raise IndexError.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Data Storage
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as contiguous memory locations for storing multiple elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: an array is a data structure that stores a fixed-size collection of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. The intuition is that it allows efficient access to elements using indices, where each index corresponds to a position in memory. Key properties: 1) Fixed size (usually defined at creation), 2) O(1) access time via index, 3) Contiguous storage. For example, an array of integers [5, 10, 15] has indices 0,1,2 storing values 5,10,15 respectively. Operations include creation (e.g., arr = [None]*5 to create an array of 5 None values), accessing (arr[0] gives first element), and modifying (arr[1] = 20 changes the second element). Time complexity for access/modify is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for n elements. This feeds into L4 as the foundation for array-based operations. Common mistakes include confusing arrays with lists (in some languages, lists are dynamic), assuming arrays can hold different types, and not understanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: In Python, lists are used as arrays; creation: arr = [0] * 3 gives [0,0,0]; access: x = arr[1]; modify: arr[2] = 5.

examples.input: Create an array of size 3 with initial values 1,2,3. Access the second element. Change the first element to 10.
examples.trace: 1. Create array: arr = [1,2,3]. Indices: 0:1, 1:2, 2:3. 2. Access second element: index 1, so arr[1] = 2. 3. Change first element: index 0, so arr[0] = 10. Final array: [10,2,3].

examples.input: Create an array of size 4 with all zeros. Set the third element to 7.
examples.trace: 1. Create array: arr = [0]*4, so [0,0,0,0]. 2. Set third element: index 2 (since zero-based), so arr[2] = 7. Final array: [0,0,7,0].
